text: A fifty-four-year-old female with history of open duodenal switch, seventeen years ago, presented with severe gastroesophageal reflux, heartburn, abdominal fullness as an occasional water brush despite therapy with ranitidine and pantoprazole daily. Also, the patient underwent an open cholecystectomy in nineteen eighty-six and an open incisional hernia repair in two thousand fourteen. Before the duodenal switch, her BMI was fifty four point eight, which decreased to forty one point nine over the first postoperative year. At present, her BMI is forty six point six. Preoperative studies were performed. An EGT showed great ED esophagitis. An upper gastrointestinal contrast study, a stricture of the duodenal jejunostomy and gastroparesis, and pH monitoring evidenced at the MRS score of seventy-seven point two.
The surgeon places a 5mm trocar in the left upper quadrant using the OptiView technique. Diagnostic laparoscopy reveals multiple adhesions between the small bowel, abdominal wall, omentum, and colon.

The surgeon carefully dissects the omentum from the abdominal wall, colon, and small bowel, revealing a dilated stomach. The omentum is separated along the shoulder line of the sleeve gastrectomy. The hiatus is dissected, creating a retroesophageal window by separating the right and left crura. A Penrose drain is passed, and the esophagus is circumferentially dissected for about ten centimeters into the mediastinum before three centimeters of the esophagus is repositioned back into the abdomen.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the lateral aspect of the sleeve and proceeds to dissect the small bowel, freeing it from the colon. The retrocholic alimentary limb is visualized and maneuvered into the supracolic area. Using a robotic vessel sealer, the surgeon accesses the lesser curvature and removes adhesions, while also taking down the right gastric artery and pyloric artery.

The surgeon transects the small bowel just below the duodenal jejunostomy using a vessel sealer and an endo-GIA tan load. A gastric pouch is created approximately five centimeters from the G-injection with an endo-GIA black load-sixty. A forty-blonde bougie is inserted into the gastric pouch to guide its formation. The remnant distal stomach and duodenal jejunostomy are placed in an endopouch and extracted through a fifteen millimeter trocar. A new gastrojejunostomy is constructed in two layers.

The surgeon completes the posterior layer of the gastrojejunostomy using 3-0 PDS sutures in a running fashion. The inner and outer layers are also constructed with running 3-0 PDS sutures. The hiatal hernia is repaired with interrupted 0 Ethibond sutures. The omentum is reattached to the lateral suture line of the gastric pouch to release tension and stabilize the pouch.
An intraoperative endoscopy reveals a wood anastomosis and a small gastric pouch, with no visible evidence of leaks. The postoperative upper GI imaging confirms the absence of leaks.
